questions.question: What is the key principle of survey report writing mentioned in the guide?

answerOptions.text: Write the report as if you will never be available to explain it
answerOptions.rationale: The guide explicitly states this principle: in two years, you probably won't be available to explain it.

answerOptions.text: Write the report as quickly as possible to meet deadlines
answerOptions.rationale: Speed is not the guiding principle; clarity, completeness, and traceability are.

answerOptions.text: Write the report using as much technical jargon as possible
answerOptions.rationale: The goal is that anyone should be able to understand what was done, not to impress with jargon.

answerOptions.text: Write the report only for the client's project manager
answerOptions.rationale: Reports serve multiple audiences and purposes including quality evidence, future reference, and contractual deliverables.

questions.hint: Think about the long-term purpose of a survey report and who might need it years later.

questions.question: When should you begin drafting the mobilisation report?

answerOptions.text: After the first survey line is completed
answerOptions.rationale: The mob report should be substantially complete before the first survey line.

answerOptions.text: Before the mobilisation starts, filling in sections as each phase completes
answerOptions.rationale: The guide recommends drafting the structure before mob starts and filling sections progressively to avoid last-minute rush.

answerOptions.text: After demobilisation, when all data is available
answerOptions.rationale: The mob report is needed before survey operations begin, not after demob.

answerOptions.text: Only when the client requests it
answerOptions.rationale: The mob report is a standard deliverable prepared proactively, not on request.

questions.hint: The guide gives specific timing advice to avoid a last-minute rush.

questions.question: What is the standard document numbering format described in the guide?

answerOptions.text: DATE-AUTHOR-DOCTYPE-VERSION
answerOptions.rationale: The convention does not use date or author in the numbering.

answerOptions.text: PROJECTCODE-VESSEL-DOCTYPE-SEQUENCE
answerOptions.rationale: The example given is PRJ001-VESSEL01-MOB-001.

answerOptions.text: CLIENT-PROJECT-REPORTTYPE-NUMBER
answerOptions.rationale: Client name is not part of the standard numbering convention described.

answerOptions.text: VESSEL-DATE-DOCTYPE-REVISION
answerOptions.rationale: The convention uses project code first, not vessel, and sequence number, not revision.

questions.question: What revision status does 'Rev A' indicate?

answerOptions.text: Draft for internal review
answerOptions.rationale: Rev 0 is the draft for internal review.

answerOptions.text: Issued for Use (final approved version)
answerOptions.rationale: Issued for Use is the final Rev 0 after all comments are resolved.

answerOptions.text: Issued for Review (IFR) to the client
answerOptions.rationale: Rev A is the first issue sent to the client for comment.

answerOptions.text: Incorporating client comments
answerOptions.rationale: Rev B incorporates client comments; Rev A is the initial client issue.

questions.hint: The revision control table maps letter revisions to specific statuses.

questions.question: What must always be stated on cross-section and profile plots?

answerOptions.text: The software version used to generate the plot
answerOptions.rationale: While useful metadata, the guide specifically warns about a different requirement.

answerOptions.text: The vertical exaggeration
answerOptions.rationale: The guide warns that unstated VE can mislead clients -- a 10x VE makes a 1 deg slope look like 10 deg.

answerOptions.text: The name of the surveyor who processed the data
answerOptions.rationale: Author attribution is for the report cover page, not individual plots.

answerOptions.text: The water temperature at the time of survey
answerOptions.rationale: Environmental data is recorded elsewhere; VE is the critical plot requirement highlighted.

questions.hint: The guide gives a specific warning about how this can mislead engineers and clients.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a standard section in the Daily Progress Report?

answerOptions.text: Equipment Status
answerOptions.rationale: Equipment Status is listed as a standard DPR section.

answerOptions.text: Planned Activities for the next 24 hours
answerOptions.rationale: Planned Activities is a standard DPR section.

answerOptions.text: Calibration procedure details and offsets
answerOptions.rationale: Calibration details belong in the calibration report or mob report, not the DPR.

answerOptions.text: Environmental Conditions
answerOptions.rationale: Environmental Conditions is a standard DPR section.

questions.hint: Think about what level of detail is appropriate for a daily report versus a calibration report.

questions.question: What is the recommended best practice for writing the DPR?

answerOptions.text: Write it from memory at the end of the shift to save time during operations
answerOptions.rationale: The guide explicitly warns against writing from memory at end of shift.

answerOptions.text: Write it as events happen, keeping a running log with times for all events
answerOptions.rationale: The guide says the DPR is often the primary record for resolving disputes about operational time and downtime.

answerOptions.text: Have the vessel master write it since they have the best overview
answerOptions.rationale: The DPR is the surveyor's responsibility, not the vessel master's.

answerOptions.text: Copy the previous day's DPR and update only the statistics
answerOptions.rationale: Each DPR should accurately record that specific day's events chronologically.

questions.hint: The DPR is often used to resolve disputes about operational time.

questions.question: What information must a calibration report contain to allow independent review?

answerOptions.text: Only the final determined offsets and a pass/fail statement
answerOptions.rationale: Offsets alone are insufficient; the full context of how they were determined is needed.

answerOptions.text: Environmental conditions, equipment details, procedure, results with residuals, and pass/fail assessment against stated acceptance criteria
answerOptions.rationale: The guide requires all these elements so an independent reviewer can assess calibration validity.

answerOptions.text: The software screenshots showing the calibration was run
answerOptions.rationale: Screenshots alone don't provide the structured information needed for independent assessment.

answerOptions.text: A signed statement from the senior surveyor confirming the calibration passed
answerOptions.rationale: A signature without supporting data doesn't allow independent verification.

questions.hint: An independent reviewer needs enough information to assess validity without contacting the author.

questions.question: What is one of the most common reasons a report is rejected by a client?

answerOptions.text: The report is too long and detailed
answerOptions.rationale: Being too detailed is not listed as a common rejection cause.

answerOptions.text: Charts missing north arrows, scale bars, or coordinate information
answerOptions.rationale: This is explicitly listed as one of the common causes for client rejection.

answerOptions.text: Using too many appendices
answerOptions.rationale: Appendices are expected and useful; too many is not listed as a rejection cause.

answerOptions.text: Submitting the report too early
answerOptions.rationale: Early submission is not a common rejection cause.

questions.hint: Think about what makes a chart usable and interpretable.

questions.question: When data values differ between the mobilisation report and the end-of-project report, what should you do?

answerOptions.text: Use the mob report values since they were written first
answerOptions.rationale: The mob report values may have been refined during operations.

answerOptions.text: Average the two values and report the mean
answerOptions.rationale: Averaging without justification is not a valid approach.

answerOptions.text: State the change explicitly with justification for why the value was refined
answerOptions.rationale: The guide says if a value changed (e.g., refined calibration), state this explicitly with justification.

answerOptions.text: Remove the conflicting value from the mob report to avoid confusion
answerOptions.rationale: Altering previously issued reports is not appropriate; transparency about changes is required.

questions.hint: Traceability and transparency are core reporting principles.

questions.question: What is the typical length range for an End-of-Project report?

answerOptions.text: 5-15 pages
answerOptions.rationale: 5-15 pages is the typical range for a calibration report.

answerOptions.text: 15-30 pages
answerOptions.rationale: 15-30 pages is the typical range for a mobilisation report.

answerOptions.text: 30-80 pages
answerOptions.rationale: The overview table states 30-80 pages for the End-of-Project report.

answerOptions.text: 100-200 pages
answerOptions.rationale: This exceeds the typical range given in the guide.

questions.hint: Check the report types overview table.

questions.question: How should coordinate information be handled across charts and tables in a report?

answerOptions.text: Use whatever format the software exports by default
answerOptions.rationale: Software defaults may be inconsistent; the guide requires deliberate consistency.

answerOptions.text: State datum and projection on every chart and coordinate table, use a consistent format throughout
answerOptions.rationale: The guide requires datum/projection on every chart, consistent format (DD.DDDDDD or DD MM SS.SSS), explicit coordinate order, and UTM zone numbers.

answerOptions.text: Only state the datum once in the introduction section
answerOptions.rationale: Datum and projection must be stated on every chart and coordinate table, not just once.

answerOptions.text: Convert all coordinates to decimal degrees for simplicity
answerOptions.rationale: The format choice depends on the project; the key requirement is consistency and clear labelling.

questions.hint: Think about what someone needs to correctly interpret a coordinate without flipping back to the introduction.
